Lecture 17: Complex Numbers ​

Topics: §5.7 — Why Complex Numbers, Rotation Matrix Obstruction, Cubic Formula Motivation, Complex Plane, Multiplication as Rotation + Scaling, Polar Form, De Moivre's Formula, Roots of Unity, Conjugation and Symmetry, Fundamental Theorem of Algebra, Complex Eigenvalues Date: May 11 – May 14, 2026

Overview ​

Lectures 14–16 built a powerful pipeline:

extcomputedet(tI−A)⟶factor⟶eigenvalues⟶spectral projections⟶value tables⟶A=PDP−1.

But this pipeline still has two restrictions:

RestrictionProblemResolution
The polynomial may not factor into linear factors over RSome real polynomials have no real rootsComplex numbers C
The minimal polynomial may have repeated factorsLagrange interpolation loses informationJordan canonical form, self-study

This lecture addresses the first restriction. Complex numbers make every polynomial factor into linear factors, so every matrix has eigenvalues over C.

Attention

Complex numbers fix the “no linear factor over R” problem. They do not fix repeated factors in the minimal polynomial.

1. The Pipeline Breaks Over R ​

1.1 A Matrix We Understand Perfectly ​

Consider the 90∘ rotation matrix

R=(0−110).

Geometrically, this matrix is completely clear:

R(10)=(01),R(01)=(−10).

It rotates every vector counterclockwise by 90∘.

Now compute

tI−R=(t1−1t),

so

det(tI−R)=t2+1.

1.2 The Factorization Step Fails ​

Over the real numbers,

t2+1

has no root. It does not factor into real linear factors.

So the pipeline stops:

t2+1⇝̸(t−λ1)(t−λ2) over R.

No real eigenvalues. No real spectral projections. No real diagonalization.

Attention

The matrix is not mysterious. The number system is too small.

The natural question is:

Can we enlarge the real numbers so that t2+1 has roots, and preferably so that every polynomial factors into linear factors?

The answer is yes: the complex numbers.

2. Why Complex Numbers Are Not Optional ​

2.1 The Quadratic Formula Is Not Enough Motivation ​

For

ax2+bx+c=0,

the quadratic formula says

x=−b±b2−4ac2a.

If

b2−4ac<0,

one might simply say: “there is no real solution.”

That makes −1 look optional. But cubic equations show that this attitude is wrong.

2.2 The Cubic Formula Forces −1 ​

Cubic equations

ax3+bx2+cx+d=0

also have a formula. Historically, this was discovered in the sixteenth century by Scipione del Ferro, Tartaglia, Cardano, and Bombelli.

The formula is complicated, but the key phenomenon is simple: even when a cubic has real roots, the formula may pass through −1 in the middle of the calculation.

A famous simple example is

x3=x.

Obviously,

x3−x=x(x−1)(x+1),

so the real solutions are

x=0,x=1,x=−1.

But when one applies the cubic formula, expressions involving −1 appear. They are not final answers; they occur inside intermediate cube roots.

::: success Even when the problem is real and the answer is real, the solution path may force us through complex numbers. :::

Bombelli’s insight was to compute formally with −1, using the rule

(−1)2=−1.

The imaginary parts can appear in the middle and cancel at the end. So −1 is not a meaningless symbol; it is part of the arithmetic needed to make formulas work.

2.3 The Lesson ​

Complex numbers are not a trick added for rare equations. They appear naturally in:

  • waves and oscillations;
  • electrical engineering and impedance;
  • quantum mechanics;
  • eigenvalue computations for real matrices.

Even when the measurement or final answer is real, the internal mathematics often lives in C.

3. The Complex Numbers ​

3.1 Definition ​

We introduce a new symbol i satisfying

i2=−1.

Proposition

Definition (Complex Number)

A complex number is an expression

z=a+bi,a,b∈R.

The number a is the real part, and b is the imaginary part.

The set of all complex numbers is denoted C:

N⊂Z⊂Q⊂R⊂C.

3.2 The Complex Plane ​

The complex number

z=a+bi

corresponds to the point

(a,b)∈R2.

The horizontal axis is the real axis. The vertical axis is the imaginary axis.

For example,

1+2i↔(1,2).

This is why C can be drawn as a plane.

3.3 Addition ​

Addition is componentwise:

(a+bi)+(c+di)=(a+c)+(b+d)i.

So addition in C is exactly vector addition in R2.

Example:

(1+2i)+(2+i)=3+3i.

The new structure is not addition. The new structure is multiplication.

3.4 Multiplication ​

To multiply, expand and use i2=−1:

(3+i)(5+i)=15+3i+5i+i2=14+8i.

In general,

(a+bi)(c+di)=(ac−bd)+(ad+bc)i.

This formula looks algebraic, but it has a geometric meaning: complex multiplication is rotation plus scaling.

4. Multiplication as Rotation and Scaling ​

4.1 Multiplication by i ​

For any complex number a+bi,

i(a+bi)=ai+bi2=−b+ai.

In coordinates,

(a,b)↦(−b,a).

This is exactly counterclockwise rotation by 90∘.

::: success Multiplication by i is the same operation as the rotation matrix

(0−110).

:::

This connects directly to the obstruction from §1. The matrix whose real characteristic polynomial was t2+1 is simply “multiplication by i” written in real coordinates.

4.2 Multiplication by a General Complex Number ​

Let

z=a+bi.

Multiplication by z is the unique real-linear transformation that sends

1↦z

and therefore sends

i↦zi.

Since multiplying by i rotates by 90∘, the two vectors z and zi are perpendicular and have the same length. Thus the unit square with vertices

0,1,i,1+i

is sent to the tilted square with vertices

0,z,zi,z(1+i).

This means multiplication by z has two geometric effects:

  1. rotate by the angle of z;
  2. scale by the length of z.

4.3 Absolute Value and Argument ​

For

z=a+bi,

define the absolute value

|z|=a2+b2.

This is the distance from 0 to z in the complex plane.

Define the argument arg⁡(z) to be the angle from the positive real axis to the vector z.

If

z=a+bi=|z|(cos⁡θ+isin⁡θ),

then

θ=arg⁡(z),a=|z|cos⁡θ,b=|z|sin⁡θ.

Proposition

Geometric Meaning of Multiplication

Multiplying by z rotates by arg⁡(z) and scales by |z|.

Special case:

i=cos⁡π2+isin⁡π2.

So multiplying by i rotates by π2 and scales by 1.

5. Polar Form and De Moivre’s Formula ​

5.1 Polar Form ​

Every nonzero complex number can be written as

z=r(cos⁡θ+isin⁡θ),

where

r=|z|>0,θ=arg⁡(z).

This is called the polar form of z.

Example:

1+i=2(cos⁡π4+isin⁡π4).

5.2 Multiplication in Polar Form ​

Let

z1=r1(cos⁡θ1+isin⁡θ1),z2=r2(cos⁡θ2+isin⁡θ2).

Then

z1z2=r1r2(cos⁡(θ1+θ2)+isin⁡(θ1+θ2)).

Thus:

|z1z2|=|z1||z2|,

and

arg⁡(z1z2)=arg⁡(z1)+arg⁡(z2).

This is the formal version of “rotation plus scaling.”

5.3 De Moivre’s Formula ​

Applying the multiplication rule repeatedly gives:

Proposition

De Moivre’s Formula

For every integer n≥1,

(r(cos⁡θ+isin⁡θ))n=rn(cos⁡(nθ)+isin⁡(nθ)).

In particular, if |z|=1, then

(cos⁡θ+isin⁡θ)n=cos⁡(nθ)+isin⁡(nθ).

Raising to the n-th power multiplies the angle by n.

5.4 Roots of Unity ​

We now solve

zn=1.

Write

z=r(cos⁡θ+isin⁡θ).

Then by De Moivre,

zn=rn(cos⁡(nθ)+isin⁡(nθ)).

For this to equal 1, we need

rn=1

and

nθ=2πk(k∈Z).

So

r=1,θ=2πkn.

The n-th roots of unity are

cos⁡2πkn+isin⁡2πkn,k=0,1,…,n−1.

They are n equally spaced points on the unit circle.

Examples:

  • The fourth roots of unity are
1,i,−1,−i.
  • If ω is a primitive third root of unity, then
1+ω+ω2=0.

6. Conjugation and Symmetry ​

6.1 Conjugate ​

For

z=a+bi,

the conjugate is

z¯=a−bi.

Geometrically, conjugation reflects the complex plane across the real axis.

Example:

1+2i―=1−2i.

6.2 Basic Identities ​

The most important identity is

zz¯=(a+bi)(a−bi)=a2+b2=|z|2.

This is always real and nonnegative.

We can also recover real and imaginary parts from z and z¯:

Re(z)=z+z¯2,

and

Im(z)=z−z¯2i.

Conjugation respects addition and multiplication:

z+w―=z¯+w¯,zw―=z¯w¯.

6.3 Division ​

Conjugation makes division possible by converting the denominator into a real number.

Example:

3+4i2+i.

Multiply top and bottom by the conjugate of the denominator:

3+4i2+i=(3+4i)(2−i)(2+i)(2−i).

The denominator is

(2+i)(2−i)=22+12=5.

The numerator is

(3+4i)(2−i)=6−3i+8i−4i2=10+5i.

So

3+4i2+i=10+5i5=2+i.

6.4 Symmetry Principle ​

Conjugation is not just a formula. It is the fundamental symmetry of C over R.

A complex number is fixed by conjugation exactly when it is real:

z¯=z⟺z∈R.

So the real numbers are the symmetric elements inside the complex numbers.

Proposition

Symmetry Principle

If a symmetric system produces an asymmetric solution, then applying the symmetry to that solution produces another valid solution. The collection of all such solutions recovers the symmetry.

For complex numbers, the symmetry is conjugation.

6.5 Real Polynomials Have Conjugate Roots ​

Let p(t) be a polynomial with real coefficients. If

p(z)=0,

then

p(z¯)=0.

Reason:

p(z¯)=p(z)―=0―=0.

Thus complex roots of real polynomials come in conjugate pairs.

Example:

t2−2t+5=0

has roots

1+2i,1−2i.

The polynomial is real and symmetric under conjugation; the roots appear as a conjugate pair.

7. Complex Eigenvalues ​

7.1 Fundamental Theorem of Algebra ​

Proposition

Fundamental Theorem of Algebra

Every degree-n polynomial with complex coefficients has exactly n complex roots, counted with multiplicity.

Equivalently,

p(t)=(t−z1)(t−z2)⋯(t−zn),zi∈C.

Therefore every polynomial factors into linear factors over C.

This removes the first obstruction from our spectral pipeline.

7.2 Every Matrix Has Complex Eigenvalues ​

For any n×n matrix A,

det(tI−A)

is a degree-n polynomial. By the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra, it has n complex roots counted with multiplicity.

So every matrix has eigenvalues over C.

If A is real, then det(tI−A) has real coefficients. Therefore non-real eigenvalues appear in conjugate pairs.

7.3 Rotation Matrix Revisited ​

Return to

R=(0−110).

We found

det(tI−R)=t2+1.

Over C,

t2+1=(t−i)(t+i).

So the eigenvalues are

i,−i.

They are a conjugate pair, as expected for a real matrix.

The spectral projections are

Pi=R−(−i)Ii−(−i)=R+iI2i,

and

P−i=R−iI−2i.

Thus

R=iPi+(−i)P−i.

Over R, the rotation matrix had no eigenvalues. Over C, the full spectral machinery runs.

7.4 Another Real Matrix with Complex Eigenvalues ​

Let

A=(12−21).

Then

tI−A=(t−1−22t−1),

so

det(tI−A)=(t−1)2+4=t2−2t+5.

Over C,

t2−2t+5=(t−(1+2i))(t−(1−2i)).

The eigenvalues are

1+2i,1−2i.

Corresponding eigenvectors are

(1i)for λ=1+2i,

and

(1−i)for λ=1−2i.

The eigenvectors also form a conjugate pair.

7.5 Symmetry of Eigenstructure ​

If A is real and

Av=λv,

then conjugating both sides gives

Av¯=λ¯v¯.

So for real matrices:

  • complex eigenvalues come in conjugate pairs;
  • complex eigenvectors come in conjugate pairs;
  • spectral projections also respect conjugation.

This is the symmetry principle applied to eigenstructure.

8. What We Have Gained ​

Complex numbers solve the factorization problem:

::: success Over C, every polynomial factors into linear factors. :::

Therefore the pipeline

det(tI−A)⟶eigenvalues⟶spectral projections⟶A=PDP−1

can now be applied to matrices whose eigenvalues are not real.

But the repeated-factor restriction remains:

Attention

If the minimal polynomial has repeated factors, Lagrange interpolation still fails. Complex numbers do not fix that. This is the territory of Jordan canonical form.

So the current usable criterion is:

  • work over C so that factorization into linear factors is always possible;
  • then check whether the minimal polynomial, or equivalently the square-free part test from Lecture 16, has distinct factors that annihilate the matrix.

9. Summary ​

::: success Key Results from This Lecture

  1. The real spectral pipeline can fail because det(tI−A) may not factor over R.

  2. Complex numbers enlarge R by adding i with i2=−1.

  3. A complex number a+bi is a point in the plane, but with multiplication.

  4. Multiplication by a complex number means rotation by its argument and scaling by its absolute value.

  5. Polar form:

z=r(cos⁡θ+isin⁡θ).
  1. De Moivre’s formula:
zn=rn(cos⁡(nθ)+isin⁡(nθ)).

  1. Conjugation is the symmetry of C over R.

  2. Real polynomials and real matrices have non-real roots/eigenvalues in conjugate pairs.

  3. By the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra, every matrix has eigenvalues over C. :::

Exercises ​

Problem

Exercise 1: Complex Arithmetic

Compute:

(a) (2+3i)+(4−i)

(b) (2+3i)(4−i)

(c) i(5−2i)

(d) 1+2i3−i

Problem

Exercise 2: Rotation by Multiplication

Let z=2+i.

(a) Compute iz.

(b) Draw z and iz in the complex plane.

(c) Verify geometrically that multiplication by i rotates by 90∘.

Problem

Exercise 3: Polar Form

Write each complex number in polar form:

(a) 1+i

(b) −1+i

(c) −2

(d) 3+i

Problem

Exercise 4: De Moivre

Use De Moivre’s formula to compute:

(a) (1+i)4

(b) (cos⁡π6+isin⁡π6)6

(c) all solutions of z4=1

Problem

Exercise 5: Conjugate Pairs

Let

p(t)=t2−4t+13.

(a) Find the roots of p(t).

(b) Verify that they are conjugates.

(c) Explain why this had to happen before computing the roots.

Problem

Exercise 6: Complex Eigenvalues

Let

A=(0−220).

(a) Compute det(tI−A).

(b) Factor it over C.

(c) Find the complex eigenvalues.

(d) Interpret the matrix geometrically as rotation plus scaling.
